narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 76% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at the lowest level—far below the typical student's mid-range performance—as well as your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 and LSAT score of 25, both notably lower than the averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen across applicants. Your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, you're Hispanic, male, and planning full-time studies, which are less common or average in the applicant pool but played smaller roles here. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile, as being in the bottom group compared to peers heavily pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which, being well below average, further tilted the odds against passing. Your Year 1 decile ranked third in impact, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles relative to typical students. Family income followed closely, with lower-middle status nudging the prediction toward failure more than higher brackets do for others. Your racial background as Hispanic had a moderate influence in the same direction, given its rarity in the dataset dominated by White applicants. Undergraduate GPA and full-time status had smaller but still negative pulls, while gender was the least influential. Overall, these factors together painted a picture riskier than the strong profiles of students who typically get approved. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT if possible to boost your score through targeted prep, build stronger academic habits shown in any graduate work, and invest in bar exam preparation courses early, as these can directly counter the model's concerns about your past performance. With dedication, you can shift this narrative and prove the prediction wrong. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
